reference:
Several methods exist in JavaScript to convert a string to title case, where the first letter of each word is capitalized. A common approach involves first converting the entire string to lowercase, then capitalizing the initial letter of each word.  This can be achieved through a `for` loop, the `map()` method, or the `replace()` method. A helper function can streamline this process for repeated use.

Using a `for` loop, you can iterate through the string, convert it to lowercase using `String.prototype.toLowerCase()`, and then selectively capitalize the first letter of each word.  Alternatively, the `map()` method allows you to transform each word in an array derived from the string, capitalizing the first letter of each element. Finally, the `replace()` method offers another route, enabling direct modification of the string.



For example, to title case the string "this is a title case string", you could employ a helper function that splits the string into words, capitalizes the first letter of each word, and joins the words back together.  This function provides a concise and reusable solution for consistently formatting strings in title case.

This article shows how to get detailed logs of the True Image Monitor's activity in Acronis True Image 2018.
True Image Monitor starts automatically with Windows and is responsible for showing icons and notifications in the system tray.
True Image Monitor's executable file, TrueImageMonitor.exe, is located in the following folder:
- Windows 64-bit: "C:\Program Files (x86)\Acronis\TrueImageHome"
- Windows 32-bit: "C:\Program Files\Acronis\TrueImageHome"
The level of logging is defined in the configuration file TrueImageMonitor.config, lying next to TrueImageMonitor.exe:
- Disable the self-defence feature of Active Protection as explained here. Otherwise, Active Protection will not allow modifying Acronis True Image configuration files.
- Locate the file TrueImageMonitor.config and copy it to the Desktop folder. That is needed because files in the folders Program Files and Program Files (x86) are protected by the operating system and cannot be edited directly there.
- Right-click the copied file in the Desktop folder and select Open with:
- Click More apps:
- Click Notepad and click OK to confirm the selection:
- Configuration file will open in Notepad text editor:
- Replace all the occurrences of level="info" with level="debug":
- Click File -> Save to save the changes and close Notepad window:
Drag and drop the file TrueImageMonitor.config from the Desktop to its original location:
Choose the Replace option when asked:
Click Continue to confirm the operation:
- Reboot the computer for changes to take effect.
In certain cases you could just restart the process TrueImageMonitor.exe instead of doing a full system reboot, but that would exclude the boot chain events logs which may be important in troubleshooting. If you are unsure, do a complete system reboot.
- Reproduce the problem.
- Detailed logs of the True Image Monitor could be found in the folder C:\ProgramData\Acronis\TrueImageHome\Logs\ti_monitor:
- Turn on Active Protection and the self-defence feature.
- If you are working with Acronis directly, generate a system report and send it to Acronis. The folder with Tray Monitor logs will be included in the report automatically.
- When the detailed logs of True Image Monitor are no longer needed, repeat the steps from this article, replacing level="debug" with level="info" at step 7.

Revised text:
reference:
To enable detailed logging for Acronis True Image Monitor in Acronis True Image 2018, follow these steps. True Image Monitor runs automatically with Windows and manages system tray icons and notifications.

First, access the configuration file. The TrueImageMonitor.exe executable is located in one of these folders, depending on your Windows version: 64-bit: "C:\Program Files (x86)\Acronis\TrueImageHome"; 32-bit: "C:\Program Files\Acronis\TrueImageHome". The associated configuration file, TrueImageMonitor.config, resides in the same folder. To edit this file, temporarily disable Acronis Active Protection's self-defense feature (refer to Acronis documentation for instructions). Then, copy TrueImageMonitor.config to your Desktop to bypass operating system restrictions on editing files in the Program Files folders. Open the copied file with Notepad.

Next, modify the logging level. Within Notepad, locate and replace all instances of "level="info"" with "level="debug"". Save the changes and close Notepad. Drag and drop the modified TrueImageMonitor.config file back into its original location, selecting “Replace” when prompted.

Finally, apply the changes and review the logs. Restart your computer for the changes to take effect, ensuring boot chain events are captured. To view detailed logs, check the following folder: C:\ProgramData\Acronis\TrueImageHome\Logs\ti_monitor. After troubleshooting, revert the logging level by repeating these steps and changing "level="debug"" back to "level="info"". If you are working directly with Acronis support, generating a system report will automatically include these logs.

Cairns to the Cape
We are excited to be partnering with Churches of Christ for our Cairns to the Cape Christmas Appeal! Help us bring joy to children, young people, foster and kinship carers across Cape York this Christmas.
What is Cairns to the Cape?
The Churches of Christ Children, Youth and Families division is based in Cairns and serves all foster children and families in Aurukun, Pormpuraaw, Kowanyama, Weipa, Napranum, Mapoon and Lockhart River. Children belong in safe and caring homes, but families are complicated and some face significant challenges that mean it's not safe for children and young people to remain at home. Even while children cannot stay at home, Churches of Christ support them to build positive, healthy relationships with their family through the committed support of foster and kinship carers.
It is thanks to these dedicated individuals and families that children and young people are able to grow and find belonging in their community. Our church is going to bless and encourage them by sending a Christmas gift hamper for each foster family!
How do I participate?
It's super simple:
- Choose a foster family from the list (or decide to buy a few items or make an optional financial donation to contribute to a hamper)
- Use the shopping list we provide to go shopping for hamper items!
- Return the gifts by Sunday 30th October
What should I buy?
We want to fill these hampers to help foster families celebrate, enjoy a shared meal and bring their family together at Christmas. Food is quite difficult to get in remote communities at the moment, so this hamper will be a big blessing.
In addition to what you buy for your hamper, we are going to buy and refrigerate a small ham for each family. Additional financial donations to Cairns to the Cape will cover this cost.
Please buy at least one thing from each list below, and feel free to buy additional items from the lists to fill the hamper. Please don't buy any items that are too big, or in quantities that prevent the lid from staying closed (the hampers are 22cm x 35cm x 29cm).
#1 An item of special Christmas food
- Tinned Christmas fruit e.g. cherries/plums
- Christmas cake & UHT Custard
- Tinned wafer biscuits/sticks
- Tinned meat or soup
#2 An item for a family snack
- Dried fruit
- Tea or coffee
#3 A game for the family to play together
- Card game
- Small board game
#4 A small gift for each child/teenager in the family
- Be mindful of the age and gender of the person you are buying for.
- For children: Book, toy, sport, hobby item, craft kit, hat, puzzle, hairbrush/comb/hair ties (gift set), bath or body lotion (gift set).
- For teenagers: headphones, speaker, comic book, jewellery, fishing item, hairbrush/comb/hair ties (gift set), bath or body lotion (gift set).
#5 Something special to celebrate Christmas
- Christmas crackers (bon bons) or decorations
- A hand-written Christmas greeting card
Frequently Asked Questions
Is there anything I am not allowed to buy?
Please do not include aerosol cans, medicine, vitamins, used clothes, anything easily breakable or food that melts easily or will perish in the next 6 months. You can wrap gifts with clear cellophane, but don't fully seal the wrapping as each item needs to be checked by staff before delivery.
Can I put in bibles, scriptures, or items about Jesus?
We want to respect and honour the reality that some Aboriginal communities may still suffer effects of historical harm by churches. Our goal is not to evangelise, but to show a simple expression of love and generosity – the recipients will know the gifts come from a church community who cares.
Will the recipients know who bought the gifts for them?
Churches of Christ workers will personally deliver the hamper you have filled to each family and explain where the gifts are from. If you include a hand-written Christmas greeting card please only include your first names (e.g. from Nick and Sarah). For any hampers made up of contributions from a number of people, we'll include a greeting card with the first names of those people.
How can I make a financial donation to cover the cost of the hams?
You can give towards this project by credit card on our website (select "Cairns to the Cape donation") or at the Info Desk on Sundays, or by placing cash in a marked envelope in a Giving Box on Sundays.
I have more questions or need help.
Contact us and we will get back to you asap.

Revised text:
reference:
**Cairns to the Cape Christmas Appeal**

We invite you to partner with Churches of Christ for our Cairns to the Cape Christmas Appeal, bringing joy to children, young people, and foster and kinship carers in Cape York this Christmas.

**What is Cairns to the Cape?**

Churches of Christ Children, Youth and Families provides essential support to foster children and families in remote communities including Aurukun, Pormpuraaw, Kowanyama, Weipa, Napranum, Mapoon, and Lockhart River. While every child deserves a safe and nurturing home, some families face challenges that make it difficult to provide that environment. Churches of Christ supports these children and families, fostering positive relationships and ensuring children maintain connections with their families through the dedication of foster and kinship carers.

To express our gratitude for these caregivers, we will provide a Christmas gift hamper to each family. 

**How You Can Participate**

Participating is easy:

1.  Select a foster family from the provided list, or contribute items or make a financial donation to support a hamper.
2.  Use the shopping list below to purchase hamper items.
3.  Return the gifts by Sunday, October 30th.

**Suggested Hamper Items**

These hampers aim to help foster families celebrate Christmas, enjoy a shared meal, and connect as a family. Food access is limited in remote communities, making these hampers especially meaningful. In addition to your purchased items, we will provide a refrigerated ham for each family. Financial donations specifically designated for “Cairns to the Cape” will cover this cost. Please choose at least one item from each category below, and feel free to add additional items to fill the hamper. Items should fit within a hamper measuring 22cm x 35cm x 29cm.

**#1 Christmas Food**
*   Tinned Christmas fruit (e.g., cherries, plums)
*   Christmas cake and UHT custard
*   Tinned wafer biscuits/sticks
*   Tinned meat or soup

**#2 Family Snack**
*   Dried fruit
*   Tea or coffee

**#3 Family Game**
*   Card game
*   Small board game

**#4 Gift for Each Child/Teenager** (Consider age and gender)
*   *Children:* Book, toy, sports item, craft kit, hat, puzzle, hairbrush/comb/hair ties, bath or body lotion.
*   *Teenagers:* Headphones, speaker, comic book, jewelry, fishing item, hairbrush/comb/hair ties, bath or body lotion.

**#5 Christmas Celebration**
*   Christmas crackers (bon bons) or decorations
*   Hand-written Christmas greeting card (include only first names)

**Frequently Asked Questions**

*   **What shouldn't I buy?** Please avoid aerosol cans, medication, vitamins, used clothing, fragile items, or perishable food. Gifts can be wrapped in clear cellophane; do not fully seal the wrapping as items need inspection before delivery.
*   **Can I include religious items?** To respect the experiences of Aboriginal communities and avoid unintended harm, we refrain from including religious materials. Our focus is on demonstrating love and generosity. Recipients will understand the gifts come from a caring church community.
*   **Will the families know who provided the gifts?** Churches of Christ staff will personally deliver hampers and explain the source of the gifts. Greeting cards should include only first names. For group contributions, a card will list the first names of contributors.
*   **How can I donate financially?** You can donate online (select "Cairns to the Cape donation"), at the Info Desk on Sundays, or by placing cash in a designated Giving Box.

**Questions?** Contact us for assistance.

Original text:
The beautiful country New Zealand is famous for its beaches with staggering beauty, its mountains covered with snow, its ancient glaciers, and its indigenous Maori culture. And if you are planning a tour to visit this small island nation, the first thing you need to know is what time is best to visit New Zealand. Well, maybe you are thinking why? Let me tell you the reason.
Arrowtown, New Zealand
New Zealand's climate varies wildly and the weather here is hard to predict. It's because New Zealand is long and narrow, it is made up of two main islands and some smaller islands. The two main islands: the North Island and the South Island are separated by Cook Strait, 22 kilometers wide at its narrowest point. Conditions vary sharply across regions from subtropical in Northland to too cold in alpine areas of the South Island.
That's why you need to figure out a suitable time to visit your destination in New Zealand before planning a trip. So let's see when the best time for your trip is.
What is the best time to visit New Zealand?
Summertime: December – February
New Zealand's summer season runs from December to February. With average high temperatures ranging from 20-24oC (68-75oF) in North Island and from 19-23oC (66-73oF) in South Island, summer in here is hot but still pleasant. Sunshine hours are long, can last up until 9.30 pm, and rain is not too common in most places.
This is the most popular time to visit New Zealand because many beaches and lakes are at their perfect time to cool off. People all over the world flock here to enjoy its great beaches with outdoor activities like swimming, beach picnics, kayaking, snorkeling, sailing… in sunlight.
Sunset on New Zealand's West Coast
Some ideal destinations for your summer vacation in New Zealand: Kaiteriteri Beach with golden sand and stunning scenery; Mt Maunganui with surf, beachside cafes and gorgeous white sand; Cathedral Cove with its unique rock arch, Piha Beach with its dramatic land and seascapes…
Tips for travelers: In peak season, please get the booking in advance for accommodation, tours, and transport. And don't forget your sunscreen with a high SPF.
Autumn: March - May
From March to May, the weather is still warm a little bit –,, particularly in the north. Temperatures range from 17 - 22oC (62 - 71oF) in North and 13 - 19oC (55 - 66oF) in South. Long, sunny days tend to linger until March and nights begin to get cooler around mid-April. Leaves on the trees start to change their colors and the sky looks so deep blue that you feel so romantic and uplifting and start to think about hiking or cycling along the trails.
Autumn is the perfect time of year to hike, cycle, or go fishing in the cool breeze. And it's still hot enough to swim in most places without having busy crowds as in summer.
For hiking, you can try the ten Great Walks with combinations of cultural significance and scenery or Mou Waho Island for its stunning views of colorful Arethusa Pool.
Wellington, New Zealand
Or you can choose to visit Wellington to try mountain biking and sea-water kayaking, as well as beautiful walks around the harbor and surrounding hills.
Tips for travelers: As autumn is also the shoulder season, you can have lots of accommodation choices with vacancies at better rates compared to summer. So my advice here is to let travel to New Zealand in autumn to enjoy fewer crowds and save a lot of money, especially on flights and accommodation.
Winter: June - August
In winter months, temperatures are much milder in New Zealand compared to other areas of similar latitude. Maxima is generally 11 - 15 °C (51 - 59 °F) in the North Island while the South Island is a bit cooler, with maximum temperatures around 7 - 12 °C (45 - 54 °F), though sometimes lower. Winter brings snow to the alpine region around the country and clear, mild not too cold days in lower regions. Now it's time for ski and snow sports in the mountainous areas, like the Central Plateau, Southern Alps, Canterbury, or Central Otago.
There are some regions that are popular with tourists in winter: Mount Ruapehu, Mount Hutt near Christchurch, and ski fields in the Queenstown and Wanaka.
Winter in Otago, New Zealand
And winter is not only for skiers, but you can also try other things here like: checking in the winter festival in Oamaru; skydiving in Queenstown, hot springs at Hanmer Springs…
Tips for travelers: The temperature difference is quite large between the top and the foot of the mountain. Snow generally falls at higher elevations and only occasionally does it reach the ground during winter. So try to wear clothes in layers because that is the best way to deal with the fluctuations in temperature and the weather, especially when you move to mountainous regions. And check the weather forecast before going to your intended destinations.
Spring: September – November
Spring is the season for all things to proliferate, and spring in New Zealand is no exception. Colorful flowers bloom, waterfalls wake up from freeze, lambs bounce around green fields. The fresh, happy atmosphere everywhere makes this also an appropriate time of year to visit New Zealand. Average daytime temperatures during spring range between 19°C (66°F) in the north to 16°C (60°F) in the south.
The weather starts to warm up, but there is still a chance of a cold snap and snow even in November. So you can expect a mixture of weather: cool nights and warm days. And it makes your travel more pleasant with outdoor activities like hiking, cycling or even skiing.
The Shire, Hobbiton
Tips for travelers: Although spring is not a peak season in New Zealand, there are school holidays that fall in spring. So if you do not like crowds, you may need to check to avoid those dates. And the weather is very variable and unpredictable during spring in New Zealand so be mentally prepared to deal with this erratic weather.
Now maybe you have your own answer to the question of what time of year is best to visit New Zealand. I personally like to go there in autumn as I mentioned above. Anyway, each time has both advantages and disadvantages. How about your opinion? Share your own experience with the comments below. See you!

NengoDL allows Keras models to be integrated directly into a NengoDL network, through the use of nengo_dl.TensorNode (see this example). However, rather than keeping a model defined in Keras/TensorFlow, sometimes we may want to convert it to a native Nengo model (composed only of Nengo objects, such as nengo.Ensemble and nengo.Connection). One reason we might want to do this is if we want to be able to run the model on different Nengo backends. Keras models can only run in the NengoDL Simulator, but a native Nengo model can run in any Nengo Simulator (including backends for specialized neuromorphic hardware). Another reason we might want to convert a Keras model to a Nengo network is so that we can use Nengo's spiking neuron types rather than the standard Keras/TensorFlow activation functions.
Whatever the motivation, the nengo_dl.Converter tool is designed to automate the translation from Keras to Nengo as much as possible. To use it, simply instantiate a Keras model and then pass it to the Converter:
inp = tf.keras.Input(shape=(28, 28, 3))
conv = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=3, activation=tf.nn.relu)(inp)
flat = tf.keras.layers.Flatten()(conv)
dense = tf.keras.layers.Dense(units=10, activation=tf.nn.relu)(flat)
model = tf.keras.Model(inputs=inp, outputs=dense)
converter = nengo_dl.Converter(model)
The Converter object will have a.net attribute which is the converted Nengo network. This can then be passed to a Nengo Simulator. For example, we could run that converted Keras network in the standard Nengo (not NengoDL) Simulator:
with nengo.Simulator( as sim:
The converter also has.inputs and.outputs attributes which can be used to look up the Nengo Nodes/Probes corresponding to the Keras model inputs/outputs. For example, to see the output of the converted network:
nengo_dl.Converter has many options that control different aspects of the conversion process. For example, we could change all the tf.nn.relu activation functions in the Keras model to spiking neurons:
converter = nengo_dl.Converter(
 model, swap_activations={tf.nn.relu: nengo.SpikingRectifiedLinear()})
We will not go into all the available options here; see the API docs for more details.
Keep in mind that the Converter is designed to automate the translation process as much as possible, but it will not work for all possible Keras networks. In general, the Converter will fall back to using TensorNodes for any elements that cannot be converted to native Nengo objects (set allow_fallback=False if you would like this to be an error instead). The Converter.verify function can be used to check that the output of the Nengo network matches the output of the Keras model.
Extending the converter
Since we know that it will not be possible to automatically translate all possible Keras models, the Converter has been designed to be easily extensible. So if you have a model containing elements that the Converter does not know how to translate, you can augment the Converter with custom conversion logic.
For example, suppose we have a model containing a custom Keras layer:
class AddOne(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
 def call(self, inputs):
 return inputs + 1
inp = tf.keras.Input(shape=(1,))
dense = tf.keras.layers.Dense(units=10)(inp)
addone = AddOne()(dense)
model = tf.keras.Model(inputs=inp, outputs=addone)
Converter would fail to convert this model to native Nengo objects, since it does not know how to translate the AddOne layer:
converter = nengo_dl.Converter(model, allow_fallback=False)
Traceback (most recent call last):
TypeError: Unable to convert layer add_one to native Nengo objects
We could set allow_fallback=True to use a TensorNode to implement the AddOne layer, but suppose we want to use native Nengo objects instead. We need to make a custom nengo_dl.converter.LayerConverter subclass, which contains the logic for translating an AddOne layer. Note that this may require some knowledge of how Keras layers work under the hood, which is not extensively documented. Your best bet may be to look at the existing LayerConverter classes to find a similar Layer type to start from.
As an example, here is how we might translate the AddOne layer:
class ConvertAddOne(nengo_dl.converter.LayerConverter):
 def convert(self, node_id):
 # create a Nengo object representing the output of this layer node
 output = self.add_nengo_obj(node_id)
 # connect up the input of the layer node
 self.add_connection(node_id, output)
 # create a node to output a constant 1 vector
 bias = nengo.Node([1] * output.size_in)
 # connect up the bias node to the output (thereby adding one to the
 # input values)
 conn = nengo.Connection(bias, output, synapse=None)
 # mark the above connection as non-trainable (since we didn't have any
 # trainable parameters in the AddOne layer, we don't want any in the
 # converted Nengo equivalent either)
 self.set_trainable(conn, False)
 return output
And now if we try to convert the original Keras model, we can see that it is successfully transformed into a native Nengo network:
converter = nengo_dl.Converter(model, allow_fallback=False)
assert converter.verify()

NengoDL enables the integration of Keras models directly into Nengo networks using `nengo_dl.TensorNode`. However, you may prefer to convert Keras models to native Nengo models—networks composed solely of Nengo objects (like `nengo.Ensemble` and `nengo.Connection`). This conversion allows models to run on diverse Nengo backends, including those for neuromorphic hardware, and facilitates the use of Nengo’s spiking neuron types instead of standard Keras/TensorFlow activation functions.

The `nengo_dl.Converter` tool streamlines this translation process. To use it, instantiate a Keras model and pass it to the Converter:

```python
inp = tf.keras.Input(shape=(28, 28, 3))
conv = tf.keras.layers.Conv2D(filters=32, kernel_size=3, activation=tf.nn.relu)(inp)
flat = tf.keras.layers.Flatten()(conv)
dense = tf.keras.layers.Dense(units=10, activation=tf.nn.relu)(flat)
model = tf.keras.Model(inputs=inp, outputs=dense)
converter = nengo_dl.Converter(model)
```

The resulting `converter.net` attribute contains the converted Nengo network, which can then be run within a Nengo Simulator. For example, a converted Keras network can execute in the standard Nengo Simulator:

```python
with nengo.Simulator() as sim:
    # ...
```

The `converter.inputs` and `converter.outputs` attributes provide access to the Nengo Nodes and Probes corresponding to the Keras model’s inputs and outputs, respectively.  The Converter offers customizable options, such as replacing activation functions. For example, `tf.nn.relu` layers can be converted to spiking neurons using `converter = nengo_dl.Converter(model, swap_activations={tf.nn.relu: nengo.SpikingRectifiedLinear()})`.  Detailed explanations of all options are available in the API documentation.

The Converter automates translation as much as possible, but may fall back to using `TensorNode` objects for elements it cannot directly convert to native Nengo objects. To treat these unsupported elements as errors, set `allow_fallback=False`.  The `Converter.verify()` function confirms that the converted Nengo network produces the same output as the original Keras model.

The Converter's design promotes extensibility. If a Keras model contains elements the Converter does not recognize, you can create custom conversion logic by subclassing `nengo_dl.converter.LayerConverter`. For instance, consider a custom Keras layer:

```python
class AddOne(tf.keras.layers.Layer):
    def call(self, inputs):
        return inputs + 1
```

Attempting to convert a model containing this layer will trigger an error: `TypeError: Unable to convert layer add_one to native Nengo objects`. To resolve this, you would create a custom `LayerConverter` subclass to handle the `AddOne` layer’s translation, potentially referencing existing LayerConverter classes for guidance.

Example: Batch Write Using AWS SDK for .NET Document Model
The following C# code example illustrates single table and multi-table batch write operations. The example performs the following tasks:
 - To illustrate a single table batch write, it adds two items to the ProductCatalog table.
 - To illustrate a multi-table batch write, it adds an item to both the Forum and Thread tables and deletes and item from the Thread table.
If you followed the steps in Creating Tables and Loading Sample Data, you already have the ProductCatalog, Forum and Thread tables created. You can also create these sample tables programmatically. For more information, see Creating Example Tables and Uploading Data Using the AWS SDK for .NET. For step-by-step instructions to test the following sample, see .NET Code Samples.
Example
Copy
using System; using System.Collections.Generic; using Amazon.DynamoDBv2; using Amazon.DynamoDBv2.DocumentModel; using Amazon.Runtime; namespace com.amazonaws.codesamples { class MidLevelBatchWriteItem { private static AmazonDynamoDBClient client = new AmazonDynamoDBClient(); static void Main(string[] args) { try { SingleTableBatchWrite(); MultiTableBatchWrite(); } catch (AmazonDynamoDBException e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } catch (AmazonServiceException e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } catch (Exception e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } Console.WriteLine("To continue, press Enter"); Console.ReadLine(); } private static void SingleTableBatchWrite() { Table productCatalog = Table.LoadTable(client, "ProductCatalog"); var batchWrite = productCatalog.CreateBatchWrite(); var book1 = new Document(); book1["Id"] = 902; book1["Title"] = "My book1 in batch write using .NET helper classes"; book1["ISBN"] = "902-11-11-1111"; book1["Price"] = 10; book1["ProductCategory"] = "Book"; book1["Authors"] = new List<string> { "Author 1", "Author 2", "Author 3" }; book1["Dimensions"] = "8.5x11x.5"; book1["InStock"] = new DynamoDBBool(true); book1["QuantityOnHand"] = new DynamoDBNull(); //Quantity is unknown at this time batchWrite.AddDocumentToPut(book1); // Specify delete item using overload that takes PK. batchWrite.AddKeyToDelete(12345); Console.WriteLine("Performing batch write in SingleTableBatchWrite()"); batchWrite.Execute(); } private static void MultiTableBatchWrite() { // 1. Specify item to add in the Thread table. Table thread = Table.LoadTable(client, "Thread"); var threadBatchWrite = thread.CreateBatchWrite(); var thread1 = new Document(); thread1["ForumName"] = "S3 forum"; thread1["Subject"] = "My sample question"; thread1["Message"] = "Message text"; thread1["KeywordTags"] = new List<string> { "S3", "Bucket" }; threadBatchWrite.AddDocumentToPut(thread1); // 2b. Specify item to delete from the Thread table. threadBatchWrite.AddKeyToDelete("someForumName", "someSubject"); // 3. Create multi-table batch. var superBatch = new MultiTableDocumentBatchWrite(); superBatch.AddBatch(forumBatchWrite); superBatch.AddBatch(threadBatchWrite); Console.WriteLine("Performing batch write in MultiTableBatchWrite()"); superBatch.Execute(); } } }

```
Example: Batch Write Operations Using the AWS SDK for .NET

This code example demonstrates batch write operations in Amazon DynamoDB using the AWS SDK for .NET Document Model API. The example showcases both single-table and multi-table batch writes.

Prerequisites

Ensure you have the ProductCatalog, Forum, and Thread tables created. These tables can be created programmatically or by following the steps in the Creating Tables and Loading Sample Data guide. Refer to the .NET Code Samples for detailed instructions on testing this example.

Code Example

The following C# code illustrates how to perform batch write operations.

using System; using System.Collections.Generic; using Amazon.DynamoDBv2; using Amazon.DynamoDBv2.DocumentModel; using Amazon.Runtime; namespace com.amazonaws.codesamples { class MidLevelBatchWriteItem { private static AmazonDynamoDBClient client = new AmazonDynamoDBClient(); static void Main(string[] args) { try { SingleTableBatchWrite(); MultiTableBatchWrite(); } catch (AmazonDynamoDBException e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } catch (AmazonServiceException e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } catch (Exception e) { Console.WriteLine(e.Message); } Console.WriteLine("Press Enter to continue"); Console.ReadLine(); } private static void SingleTableBatchWrite() { Table productCatalog = Table.LoadTable(client, "ProductCatalog"); var batchWrite = productCatalog.CreateBatchWrite(); // Add a new item. var book1 = new Document(); book1["Id"] = 902; book1["Title"] = "My book1 in batch write using .NET helper classes"; book1["ISBN"] = "902-11-11-1111"; book1["Price"] = 10; book1["ProductCategory"] = "Book"; book1["Authors"] = new List<string> { "Author 1", "Author 2", "Author 3" }; book1["Dimensions"] = "8.5x11x.5"; book1["InStock"] = new DynamoDBBool(true); book1["QuantityOnHand"] = new DynamoDBNull(); batchWrite.AddDocumentToPut(book1); // Delete an existing item. batchWrite.AddKeyToDelete(12345); Console.WriteLine("Performing single-table batch write."); batchWrite.Execute(); } private static void MultiTableBatchWrite() { // Add an item to the Forum table. Table forum = Table.LoadTable(client, "Forum"); var forumBatchWrite = forum.CreateBatchWrite(); var forum1 = new Document(); forum1["Name"] = "Test BatchWrite Forum"; forum1["Threads"] = 0; forumBatchWrite.AddDocumentToPut(forum1); // Add an item to the Thread table. Table thread = Table.LoadTable(client, "Thread"); var threadBatchWrite = thread.CreateBatchWrite(); var thread1 = new Document(); thread1["ForumName"] = "S3 forum"; thread1["Subject"] = "My sample question"; thread1["Message"] = "Message text"; thread1["KeywordTags"] = new List<string> { "S3", "Bucket" }; threadBatchWrite.AddDocumentToPut(thread1); // Delete an item from the Thread table. threadBatchWrite.AddKeyToDelete("someForumName", "someSubject"); // Combine batch writes for multiple tables. var superBatch = new MultiTableDocumentBatchWrite(); superBatch.AddBatch(forumBatchWrite); superBatch.AddBatch(threadBatchWrite); Console.WriteLine("Performing multi-table batch write."); superBatch.Execute(); } } }
```

Exercises for Programmers (Python): 7 of 57 - Area of a Rectangular Room
2nd September 2020
Exercise 7 in book Exercises for Programmers: 57 Challenges to Develop Your Coding Skills by Brian P. Hogan.
Create a program that calculates the area of a room. Prompt the user for the length and width in feet. Then display the area in both square feet and square meters.
Example Output
What is the length of the room in feet? 25
What is the width of the room in feet? 20
You entered dimensions of 15 feet by 20 feet.
The area is
300 square feet
27.871 square meters
This one was straight forward for me as it can be solved quite tidily with an Object. Create an object providing the length and width values at construction time then it's easy to have accessor functions that provide the area in square feet and square meters. Of course starting with a couple of tests.
import unittest
from exercises.Ex07_area_of_a_rectangular_room.rectangular_room import RectangularRoom
class TestRectangularRoom(unittest.TestCase):
 def test_given15FeetBy20Feet_thenArea300SquareFeet(self):
 rectangularRoom = RectangularRoom(15, 20)
 self.assertEqual(rectangularRoom.areaFeet(), 300)
 def test_given15FeetBy20Feet_thenArea27point871SquareMeters(self):
 rectangularRoom = RectangularRoom(15, 20)
 self.assertEqual(rectangularRoom.areaMeters(), 27.871)
Writing tests first is a nice way to approach a problem because it drives the design. In this case I have made a clear expectation that a RectangularRoom class is required and how I expect it to take the length and width values. At this point I realise I don't know how to write a constructor for a Python class nor how to represent class variables, so some research is required and of course it turns out to be simple enough.
class RectangularRoom:
 lengthFeet = 0.0
 widthFeet = 0.0
 def __init__(self, length, width):
 self.lengthFeet = length
 self.widthFeet = width
Then a couple of functions are required to provide the area of the room in square feet and square meters. Nothing particularly tricky here other than having to figure out how to round floating point numbers to 3 decimal places.
SQUARE_FEET_TO_SQUARE_METER_CONVERSION = 0.09290304
 def areaFeet(self):
 return self.lengthFeet * self.widthFeet
 def areaMeters(self):
 return round(self.areaFeet() * self.SQUARE_FEET_TO_SQUARE_METER_CONVERSION, 3)
The script functionality with user input is nothing new from the previous examples, just read in some data, process it, and respond appropriately.
if __name__ == "__main__":
 length = input("What is the length of the room in feet? ")
 width = input("What is the width of the room in feet? ")
 print("You entered dimensions of " + length + " feet by " + width + " feet.")
 print("The area is")
 room = RectangularRoom(float(length), float(width))
 print(str(room.areaFeet()) + " square feet")
 print(str(room.areaMeters()) + " square meters.")

Revised text:
reference:
```python
import unittest

# Constants
SQUARE_FEET_TO_SQUARE_METER_CONVERSION = 0.09290304


class RectangularRoom:
    """
    Represents a rectangular room with length and width in feet,
    providing methods to calculate area in square feet and square meters.
    """

    def __init__(self, length, width):
        """
        Initializes a RectangularRoom object.

        Args:
            length (float): The length of the room in feet.
            width (float): The width of the room in feet.
        """
        self.lengthFeet = length
        self.widthFeet = width

    def areaFeet(self):
        """
        Calculates the area of the room in square feet.

        Returns:
            float: The area in square feet.
        """
        return self.lengthFeet * self.widthFeet

    def areaMeters(self):
        """
        Calculates the area of the room in square meters, rounded to three decimal places.

        Returns:
            float: The area in square meters.
        """
        return round(self.areaFeet() * SQUARE_FEET_TO_SQUARE_METER_CONVERSION, 3)


class TestRectangularRoom(unittest.TestCase):
    """
    Unit tests for the RectangularRoom class.
    """

    def test_area_in_square_feet(self):
        """
        Tests the areaFeet method with dimensions 15 feet by 20 feet.
        """
        room = RectangularRoom(15, 20)
        self.assertEqual(room.areaFeet(), 300)

    def test_area_in_square_meters(self):
        """
        Tests the areaMeters method with dimensions 15 feet by 20 feet.
        """
        room = RectangularRoom(15, 20)
        self.assertEqual(room.areaMeters(), 27.871)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    """
    Prompts the user for room dimensions, calculates the area in square feet and square meters,
    and displays the results.
    """
    length = input("What is the length of the room in feet? ")
    width = input("What is the width of the room in feet? ")

    try:
        length = float(length)
        width = float(width)
    except ValueError:
        print("Invalid input. Please enter numeric values for length and width.")
        exit()

    room = RectangularRoom(length, width)
    print(f"You entered dimensions of {length} feet by {width} feet.")
    print(f"The area is:")
    print(f"{room.areaFeet()} square feet")
    print(f"{room.areaMeters()} square meters.")
```

How to resolve: TeamViewer is unable to initialize the display parameters
 - TeamViewer hangs during the "Initialize display settings" phase in Windows 10.
 - The cause of this problem is still unclear.
 - It could be due to the quality of your internet connection, conflicts in the process, improper remote access configuration, and many other factors.
 - To solve the problem, you can try to update the problem for both users or reinstall it with Unattended Remote Access or change the Remote Access configuration.
 
Unlock the potential of TeamViewer by conquering the frustrating hurdle of initializing display parameters. In this article, we unveil effective solutions to resolve the vexing issue, allowing you to seamlessly connect and collaborate with your team.
Troubleshooting steps for resolving TeamViewer display parameter initialization issues
To troubleshoot TeamViewer display parameter initialization issues, follow these steps:
1. Open TeamViewer and ensure that both the client and host versions are up to date. Check for any available updates and install them.
2. If you encounter an error during initialization, make sure your internet connection is stable. Try restarting your router or connecting to a different network.
3. If the issue persists, try connecting to a different computer to see if the problem is specific to one device. If the display parameters initialize correctly on another computer, the issue may be with the initial computer's settings.
4. Check for any hotfixes or patches released by TeamViewer's development team. Visit their website or contact their support for assistance.
5. If you are still experiencing issues, try uninstalling and reinstalling TeamViewer. Make sure to download the latest version from the official website.
By following these troubleshooting steps, you should be able to resolve TeamViewer's display parameter initialization issues and get back to remote accessing and controlling your computer without any problems.
Updating TeamViewer and adjusting display settings to fix initialization hang
To resolve the issue of TeamViewer being unable to initialize display parameters, follow these steps:
1. Update TeamViewer to the latest version by downloading it from the official website.
2. Adjust the display settings on your PC by right-clicking on the desktop and selecting "Display settings."
3. In the Display settings window, ensure that the resolution and scaling options are set correctly for your monitor.
4. If the issue persists, try using the TeamViewer Cleanup tool to remove any traces of previous installations.
5. Restart your computer and open TeamViewer again.
If you're still experiencing the initialization hang, there may be other factors at play, such as the connection quality or compatibility issues. In such cases, it's recommended to reach out to the TeamViewer support team for further assistance. They can provide you with additional troubleshooting methods or hotfixes to resolve the issue. Remember to provide them with relevant information such as your client version and any error messages you encounter during the initialization process.
Reinstalling TeamViewer with unattended remote access and configuring computer for remote control
To resolve the "TeamViewer Unable to Initialize Display Parameters" error and reinstall TeamViewer with unattended remote access, follow these steps:
1. Uninstall TeamViewer from your computer.
2. Download the latest version of TeamViewer from the official website.
3. Install TeamViewer using the downloaded setup file.
4. Launch TeamViewer and go to the "Extras" menu.
5. Select "Options" and navigate to the "Advanced" tab.

To resolve TeamViewer initialization problems related to display parameters, you can disable conflicting processes and standby mode. These issues may cause the Initializing Display Parameters Window to hang or result in a black or blank screen during the connection attempt.

Italianate Style
historic view of Fort Mason commanding officer's residence
Fort Mason's commanding officer's residence, constructed in 1877 in the Italianate style.
History of the Style
The Italianate style, most prevalent in America between the 1850s and the 1880s, was inspired by rambling, informal Italian farmhouses and Italian-style villa architecture. The Italianate style placed an emphasis on the vertical orientation of the building, as if someone was pulling the top of the building up, and as if the building was made of putty, as it stretched upwards, the windows and door frames became narrower and attenuated. The dominant characteristic of the Italianate style was the low, overhanging roof with decorative eave brackets; there was often much decorative detail applied to the building just at the roof line. The windows were accentuated with thick, decorative hoods and the front entrances were often highlighted with a tower, a cupola or a projecting porch.
Italianate buildings were more playful and decorative than the earlier Greek Revival buildings and their floor plans designs were often asymmetrical. This style's popularity spread quickly across the country, partially as a result of newly-available building pattern books. These books, once considered too costly to reproduce, were now less expensive so families and builders alike could use them as reference manuals in designing their own buildings.
Italianate buildings at Golden Gate
While the civilian Italianate buildings in San Francisco were often wildly colorful and ornately decorated, the army's version of Italianate represented a more restrained and paired-down style. In both cases at the park - the Fort Mason Officers' Club and the Presidio Sutler's Buildingthe buildings were unique and not constructed from the standard army quartermaster plans. These two buildings were also originally constructed as residences and were both located in a highly visible part of their post.
The Fort Mason Officers' Club (Building 1), located on Franklin Street at Fort Mason, was originally built as the post's commanding officers' residence in 1877. This Italianate building has a hipped roof that extends past the buildings sides, with wooden decorated brackets. The windows are long and narrow and are outlined on all sides with thick moldings.
The Presidio Sutler's Building (Building 116) was built in 1885 as both a store and residence for the post's sutler, the man who sold goods and supplies to the soldiers. This building, located on Sheridan Road, has a hipped roof with a central, pedimented (triangular) element, decorative frieze under the roof line and a series of double pairs of multi-paned windows with decorative moldings. The building's design emphasizes its verticality.
(Return to the Guide of Architectural Styles at Golden Gate).
Fort Mason Officers' Quarters
The Italianate elements of the Fort Mason Officers' Club:
1. Overhanging roof with decorative eave brackets
2. Thick, decorative window moldings
3. Decorative frieze band at 2nd floor level
4. Projecting wood-frame "porte cochere" (car port)
historic photo of the Presidio sutlers building
This circa 1938 photo of the Presidio Sutler's Building shows the building's original Italianate features and the style's emphasis on verticality.

## Italianate Style at Golden Gate

The commanding officer’s residence at Fort Mason, constructed in 1877, exemplifies the Italianate style, a popular architectural trend in America from the 1850s to the 1880s. Inspired by informal Italian farmhouses and villas, Italianate buildings typically feature a low, overhanging roof adorned with decorative brackets, elongated windows, and often, towers, cupolas, or projecting porches. This style distinguished itself with asymmetrical designs and playful decorative elements, diverging from the earlier, more formal Greek Revival architecture. The increased availability of affordable building pattern books facilitated the widespread adoption of Italianate throughout the country.

While San Francisco’s civilian Italianate structures showcased vibrant colors and elaborate ornamentation, military applications, such as those within Golden Gate National Recreation Area, adopted a more restrained aesthetic. Notably, both the Fort Mason Officers' Club and the Presidio Sutler's Building were unique constructions, not derived from standard army quartermaster plans, and initially served as residences in prominent locations.

A circa 1938 photograph of the Presidio Sutler’s Building highlights the building’s original Italianate details and the style’s characteristic vertical emphasis.

How To Fix The Garbled Code On Notepad Files

Summary: Usually, we will record some information in a notepad, such as passwords, keys, ideas, notes, etc. But sometimes, when we open it after a while, we find many garbled characters inside. What should we do? This article will show how to fix the garbled code on notepad files.
When working extensively with Plain Text files with the TXT file extension, individuals may encounter documents with garbled text instead of the expected content. This issue often occurs when the corrupted text document is written in a non-Latin alphabet-based foreign language. Still, it can happen with any file if there are inconsistencies in the settings used during the file's saving process.
To solve the garbled code on notepad files, you must first understand the cause of the problem. The primary reason is the wrong document format. Generally, there are several reasons for garbled characters:
Methods to Fix the Garbled Code on Notepad Files
Method 1: Convert the File Extension
First, let's solve the problem caused by the file format disorder. Just change the file extension back to the original format. For example, if your file is originally a word file, you can change its file extension to .doc or .docx. If it is a web page file, change it to .html, etc. Then open these files with the appropriate program.
Method 2: Change the Encoding Option
Mismatched encoding formats for text files can also lead to garbled code. In such case, you need to change the encoding form as follows:
 1. Open the garbled file with Notepad and select Save>Save As.
 2. Choose ANSI or UTF-8 as encoding format and click Save.
Method 3: Change the System Locale
When opening various text documents with Notepad results in gibberish, it's typically a system issue. The problem could be that the encoding used for the file is not compatible with the native character encoding used by the version of Windows you have.
So if the previous method doesn't work, we can change the default system language in Windows.
 1. Search control panel in the search bar and open it.
 2. Click on Change date, time or number formats under Clock and Region.
 3. Select the Administrative tab and click on Change system locale option.
 4. In the next window, choose the language you need from the drop-down menu and click OK.
 5. Close Control Panel and restart your computer to make the changes effective.
Method 4: Use Restore Previous Version
You will also get a Notepad garbled codes issue when a Notepad file gets corrupted. Several common reasons can cause Notepad file corruption or damage, such as file header corruption, improper system shutdown, virus attacks, and incomplete download and compression issues.
Many Windows users enable Windows backup tools such as file history to protect data security and regularly back up their files. If this is the case for you, here are some steps you can take when the above two methods fail:
 1. Go to the location where the Notepad file is stored.
 2. Right-click on the corrupted file and select Previous Version from the context menu.
 3. Choose the correct previous version and click on Restore.
 4. Now, open the Notepad file to see if the problem is fixed.
Method 5: Use Microsoft Word to Repair Notepad Files
 1. Open the Word app, then click File and select Options.
 2. Shift to the Advanced tab and navigate to the General section.
 3. Check Confirm file format conversion on open and click OK to save changes.
 4. Click File and select Open.
 5. When the Open window appears, hit the Browse button.
 6. Choose All Files and select Recover Text from Any File (*.*).
 7. Select the corrupted notepad file from the list and click Open.
 8. Now, you can check whether this method helps to repair the text file.
Method 6: Open Notepad Files in the Browser
The simplest and most effective way to view the original file is by opening it with a web browser. Web browsers are designed to translate data between encoding schemes. You can use either Microsoft Edge or Google Chrome to open the text file, and it will display the language correctly.
Conclusion 
The garbled code of the Notepad file is not a big problem. When you encounter garbled code on Notepad files, the six recommended methods in this article should solve the problem in no time. If you have any other valuable methods or suggestions, welcome to leave a comment below.

**Recovering Readable Text in Notepad Files**

Notepad is a convenient tool for storing various information, but occasionally, files open with unreadable, garbled characters. This issue typically stems from inconsistencies in file formatting or encoding, but several straightforward solutions can restore the original text.

**Understanding the Cause**

Garbled text usually arises from a mismatch between the file's intended format and how it’s being displayed. This can happen when a file is saved with an incorrect extension or an incompatible encoding.

**Troubleshooting Steps**

Here are several methods to resolve this problem:

1.  **Verify the File Extension:** If a file was originally created in a program like Microsoft Word, ensure its extension matches the original format (e.g., .doc or .docx). Changing the extension and reopening the file with the appropriate application can often resolve the issue.

2.  **Adjust the Encoding:** Notepad's encoding settings significantly impact how text is displayed. To correct this:
    *   Open the garbled file in Notepad.
    *   Select *Save As*.
    *   Experiment with different encoding options, such as ANSI or UTF-8, and save the file.

3.  **Modify System Locale:** System-level encoding settings can also contribute to garbled text. If other troubleshooting methods fail, consider changing your system's default language:
    *   Search for "Control Panel" and open it.
    *   Navigate to *Clock and Region > Change date, time or number formats.*
    *   Select the *Administrative* tab, then click *Change system locale*.
    *   Choose a different language and click *OK*.
    *   Restart your computer.

4.  **Restore a Previous Version:** If file corruption is suspected, Windows' file history feature can recover earlier, uncorrupted versions:
    *   Locate the affected file.
    *   Right-click the file and select *Previous Versions*.
    *   Choose a recent version and click *Restore*.

5.  **Repair with Microsoft Word:** Microsoft Word can sometimes extract readable text from damaged Notepad files:
    *   Open Microsoft Word.
    *   Go to *File > Options > Advanced*.
    *   Check the box *Confirm file format conversion on open*.
    *   Click *OK*.
    *   Select *File > Open*, then click *Browse*.
    *   Change the file type to *Recover Text from Any File (*.*)* and select the Notepad file.

6.  **Open in a Web Browser:** Web browsers, such as Microsoft Edge or Google Chrome, often handle encoding automatically. Opening the Notepad file directly in a browser can display the text correctly.



By systematically exploring these solutions, you can typically restore readable text to your Notepad files.

RPC Specification
Topics
Jump to a Topic
Caution
This is documentation for using Blitz queries and mutations in a non-Blitz app. For example, from a mobile application or some other app.
For using queries and mutations in your app, refer to Using Queries and Using Mutations.
If you want to access your app e.g. as an external API, take a look at API Routes instead.
Request Details
From an HTTP perspective there is no difference between a query and mutation. They are both function calls that get transformed into POST requests.
Warms up the lambda. For example, when a component renders on the client that uses a mutation, it will issue a HEAD request to the mutation endpoint immediately on page load so that there's a much higher chance the actual mutation request will hit a warm lambda.
Must always return HTTP 200 OK
HEAD Responses
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
POST
Executes the query or mutation.
Request body must be JSON with the following keys:
 - params (required) - This will be provided as the first argument to the query or mutation
 - version (optional) - String containing the version of the built RPC client
Response body is JSON with the following keys:
 - result - the exact result returned from the query/mutation function or null if there was an error
 - error - null if success, otherwise an object.
Example POST body:
{
 "params": {
 "where": {
 "id": 1
 }
 }
}
POST Responses
Success
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
{
 "result": {
 "name": "Hello World",
 "description": "This is awesome :)"
 },
 "error": null
}
Bad Request (Not JSON)
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
Content-Type: application/json
{
 "result": null,
 "error": {
 "message": "Request body is not valid JSON"
 }
}
Bad Request (Missing params)
HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request
Content-Type: application/json
{
 "result": null,
 "error": {
 "message": "Request body is missing the 'params' key"
 }
}
Not Found (Wrong method)
HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found
Versioning
Any time you have client-server communication, there is a very real risk of the client & server code becoming out of sync and causing an error. For example, let's say you have a mutation function whose input is a single object. You decide to change the mutation input to be a single array. You make the change and deploy it. Your server code is now updated, but all your users still have the old client code running in the browser. So it's likely you now have client code sending an object to your mutation which is going to error because it's expecting an array.
Now one solution is to only make changes that are backwards compatible, but sometimes you can't do this.
The other solution is to track the version of your client code and your server code. If there is a version mismatch, you can display a friendly message to the user, telling them they need to reload the webpage to continue.
// *: example of an RPC call shielded by a friendly message to the user

reference:
## Integrating Blitz Queries and Mutations in Non-Blitz Applications

This documentation explains how to utilize Blitz queries and mutations within applications outside the Blitz framework, such as mobile applications or external systems. For guidance on using these features within a Blitz application, please refer to the dedicated “Using Queries” and “Using Mutations” documentation. To expose your application as an external API, explore API Routes instead.

### Request Structure and Functionality

From an HTTP perspective, queries and mutations function identically: both are invoked as function calls and translated into POST requests. To ensure efficient execution, a HEAD request is sent to the mutation endpoint during client-side component rendering. This proactively warms the Lambda function, increasing the likelihood of subsequent mutation requests reaching a warm instance.  All requests must return an HTTP 200 OK status.

### Request Details

To execute a query or mutation, send a POST request with a JSON body containing the following keys:

*   **params** (required): The arguments passed to the query or mutation function.
*   **version** (optional): A string indicating the version of the RPC client.

The response body will also be JSON and contain:

*   **result**: The returned value from the query or mutation function, or null if an error occurred.
*   **error**:  Null on success; otherwise, an object describing the error.

**Example POST Body:**

```json
{
  "params": {
    "where": {
      "id": 1
    }
  }
}
```

### Response Codes and Examples

**Success (HTTP/1.1 200 OK):**

```http
Content-Type: application/json
```

```json
{
  "result": {
    "name": "Hello World",
    "description": "This is awesome :)"
  },
  "error": null
}
```

**Bad Request (Invalid JSON) (HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request):**

```http
Content-Type: application/json
```

```json
{
  "result": null,
  "error": {
    "message": "Request body is not valid JSON"
  }
}
```

**Bad Request (Missing 'params' Key) (HTTP/1.1 400 Bad Request):**

```http
Content-Type: application/json
```

```json
{
  "result": null,
  "error": {
    "message": "Request body is missing the 'params' key"
  }
}
```

**Not Found (Incorrect Method) (HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found):**

### Managing Version Compatibility

Client-server code version mismatches are a common source of errors in communication. Consider a scenario where a mutation function's input changes from a single object to a single array. Deploying the updated server-side code while clients retain the older code will result in errors due to incompatible input types.

While maintaining backward compatibility is ideal, it's not always feasible. An alternative approach is to track the version of both your client and server code. When a version mismatch occurs, display a user-friendly message instructing them to reload the webpage to resolve the incompatibility.

When generating data tables with the API, responses sometimes truncate prematurely, indicating the table continues beyond the provided output. This limitation stems from the model's token constraints. To retrieve complete tables from longer datasets, divide the data into smaller segments and process them sequentially.

Here’s a method to accomplish this:

```python
def create_data_table(data_list):
    chunk_size = 10  # Adjust based on your needs
    chunks = [data_list[i:i+chunk_size] for i in range(0, len(data_list), chunk_size)]
    result = ""
    for chunk in chunks:
        message = f"Create a data table with the following rows:\n{', '.join(chunk)}"
        response = ask_gpt_question(message, "Create a data table with the following rows:")
        parsed_text = response.strip()
        result += parsed_text
    return result
```

This code divides the input data into smaller chunks, sends each chunk to the API, and combines the resulting table segments into a single, complete table.  Experiment with the `chunk_size` to optimize performance and ensure complete output. For tasks requiring uninterrupted output and to avoid incomplete results, consider using the full GPT-4 model (e.g., `gpt-4-0613`) or GPT-3.5-turbo-0301.

This is a statistic in boxing where it keeps track of the boxer's record. It will go in order of their wins, losses, draws, and no decisions. Typically this is an important statistic because it helps boxers to understand where they rank against others.
Points are a statistic that is awarded to a boxer from a single judge.
In boxing, a streak is a statistic that keeps track of how many matches a boxer has won or lost in a row.
A disqualification in boxing is when a boxer has committed several fouls, a major infringement, or is not following the rules.
A Technical Knockout occurs when the fight is stopped for reasons outside of the norm (a knockout, a round being over, etc.). Instead, the referee or doctor decides that the match needs to be stopped.
This is a statistic of boxing that occurs when an opponent is struck by their opponent and their knee or glove touches the mat.

Beaten Opponents
This statistic shows how many opponents a boxer has beaten.
This statistic shows how many fights a boxer has been in.
Beaten Champions
This statistic shows how many opponents a boxer has beaten that have been highly ranked or that have been champions for their weight class.
Unanimous decision in boxing is when all of the judges decide the same boxer wins the match.
Split Decision in boxing occurs when one judge believes one boxer won, but two judges thought the other boxer won.
Majority Decision in boxing is when one of the judges saw neither of the boxers win, but 2 of the judges saw one boxer win.
Technical Decision is a statistic in boxing where an accidental foul has been committed. This causes the fight to be paused. If there are at least four rounds that have been completed, the boxer who has the most points from the judges will be declared the winner. On the other hand, if the both boxers have the same amount of points after four rounds, then the match is concluded as a technical draw. But, if four rounds have not been completed, then the match is concluded as a no decision.
No Decision in boxing can also be called no contest. This statistic occurs when a fight is wiped off the record. This occurs in unusual circumstances such as when a boxer isn't following the rules, police get involved, or there is bad weather.
Referee Technical Decision is a statistic in boxing where a boxer typically decides in-between rounds that they no longer want to continue the fight. This can occur if the boxer doesn't have a desire to compete or it is decided that their safety is at risk.

Lastly, there is another kind of draw, and is called a majority draw. There are two ways this could happen. For one of them, it can occur when two judges decide there is a draw and one judge decides there is a win. For the other majority draw, two of the judges would decide there is a draw, and one judge would decide it is a loss.

Weight is a statistic in boxing that helps determine the weight classes. When two boxers compete against each other they have to be in the same weight class. Punch Totals

Power Shots Landed
This statistic is used in boxing to calculate the power shots that are thrown by a boxer. This statistic helps to separate the punches that add to the boxer's points from jabs.
Percent Landed

reference:
## Understanding Boxing Statistics

Boxing utilizes a variety of statistics to evaluate a boxer's skill and performance. These statistics provide insights into a boxer’s record, fight outcomes, and overall effectiveness. Here's a breakdown of common boxing statistics and their meanings:

**Fight Outcomes**

*   **Knockout (KO):** A KO occurs when a boxer is knocked down and unable to stand before the referee finishes the count of ten.
*   **Technical Knockout (TKO):** A TKO happens when the referee or a ringside doctor stops the fight due to a boxer’s inability to continue safely, even if the boxer hasn’t been knocked down.
*   **Draw (D):** A draw indicates no winner. This can result from unanimous scoring, conflicting judge's scores, or a majority draw where judges have differing outcomes.
*   **Technical Decision (TD):** A TD is awarded when a fight is stopped due to an accidental foul and at least four rounds have been completed; the boxer with the higher score wins. If scores are tied, the fight is a technical draw. If fewer than four rounds have passed, the result is a No Decision.
*   **No Decision (ND):** A No Decision, also known as a "no contest," removes the fight from a boxer’s record due to unusual circumstances, such as rule violations, police intervention, or extreme weather.
*   **Referee Technical Decision (RTD):** This occurs when a boxer, typically between rounds, signals their inability or unwillingness to continue, often due to injury or safety concerns.

**Record and Performance**

*   **Wins/Losses/Draws:** This standard record displays a boxer’s number of wins, losses, and draws, providing a general overview of their career.
*   **Streak:** A streak tracks consecutive wins or losses, indicating a boxer’s current momentum.
*   **Disqualification (DQ):** A DQ results from a boxer committing repeated fouls or major rule infractions.
*   **Knockdown:** Records the number of times a boxer has been knocked down during a fight.
*   **Foul:** Indicates a violation of boxing rules, potentially leading to point deductions.
*   **Beaten Opponents:** The number of opponents a boxer has defeated.
*   **Fights:** The total number of professional fights a boxer has participated in.
*   **Beaten Champions:** The number of championship-level opponents a boxer has defeated.
*   **Unanimous Decision (UD):** All judges score the fight in favor of the same boxer.
*   **Split Decision (SD):** Judges deliver differing scores, with one judge favoring one boxer and two favoring the other.
*   **Majority Decision (MD):** Two judges score the fight for one boxer, while one judge scores it for the other.
*   **Outpoint:** A boxer who consistently lands more punches than their opponent, ultimately winning by points.
*   **Punch Totals:** The total number of punches thrown by a boxer in a fight.
*   **Power Shots Landed:** Records the number of impactful, scoring punches successfully delivered.
*   **Percent Landed:**  A boxer's punching accuracy, representing the percentage of punches that connect with their opponent.

**Weight Classes**

Boxers compete within specific weight classes to ensure fair competition. Examples include: Minimum (105 lbs), Light Flyweight (108 lbs), Flyweight (112 lbs), and Heavyweight (no weight limit).

Hide the date from the taskbar in Windows 7
By default, Windows 7 displays the date next to the time in the notification area of the taskbar (system tray). This used to be the case with previous versions of Windows when you resized the taskbar to a large enough height to allow enough space to show the date, and even the day of the week. In this tutorial, we will show you how to hide the date from the Windows 7 taskbar, without having to hide the time! This means that you will keep the system clock, but only show the time, without a date.
Note that this tutorial assumes that you want to hide the date from the taskbar, but that you still want to keep the time showing in the notification area; if this is not the case, Windows 7 lets you show or hide the clock altogether, and completely remove it from the taskbar.
Show only time in taskbar, no date
To hide the date from the taskbar, follow these steps:
- Right-click on an empty area of the taskbar, and choose "Properties"
- Windows 7 will open the "Taskbar and Start Menu Properties" window
- Make sure that the Taskbar tab is selected
- When, as is the case by default, Windows 7 uses "Large Icons" in the taskbar, it makes your taskbar higher, as shown on the screenshot below:
- In the Taskbar properties window, check the "Use small icons" checkbox
- Now, click Apply to preview the change; Windows 7 will make the icons smaller, and at the same time make the taskbar itself smaller. As you noticed, the taskbar no longer displays the date, but it still displays the time:
- And, best of all, even if the date is now hidden from the taskbar, you can still see it by moving your mouse cursor above the clock, and leave it there for a second or so:
And this is all it takes to hide the date from the Windows 7 taskbar! You can at any time get the date by moving your mouse above the system clock, or by quickly resizing (temporarily) the taskbar, to get not only the date, but also the day of the week:
Tip: you can also view a full month calendar in the taskbar (instead of just a simple day or date), by giving a single click on the system clock. Some older versions of Windows used to accept a double-click, which would show the full date and time settings, but since Windows Vista you have this mini built-in calendar, right in your taskbar.up ↑

## Hiding the Date on the Windows 7 Taskbar

Windows 7 displays the date alongside the time in the taskbar’s notification area. To remove only the date while retaining the time, follow these simple steps.

Right-click an empty area of the taskbar and select “Properties.” This opens the "Taskbar and Start Menu Properties" window. Ensure the “Taskbar” tab is selected. By default, Windows 7 uses large icons, which display the date. To hide the date, check the "Use small icons" box within the Taskbar properties.

Applying this change immediately reduces the size of the icons and the taskbar itself, removing the date display while preserving the time. Hovering your mouse cursor over the time will briefly reveal the date.

You can also access a full calendar by clicking the time display directly. This convenient feature provides a detailed monthly view, a change from earlier Windows versions which required a double-click for similar functionality.

How Often Do Lions Need To Eat?
Lions are believed to feed every three or four days, and need on average between 5kg and 7kg of meat a day.
But they can go without food for more than a week and then tear into prey, eating up to 50kg of meat at a time – that's almost a quarter of the animal's body weight.
How long can a lion go without eating?
14 days
How much food does a lion need per day?
Big Cats Require Big Calories
Studies of wild lions have estimated that they eat between 10 and 25 pounds of food per day. The Association of Zoos and Aquariums recommends slightly less for captive animals, and suggests that the average male requires 8 pounds of food per day, while females require about 6 pounds.
What does a lion need to survive?
Their tan color allows lions to blend in with the savannas, open woodlands and deserts in which they live. Lions primarily hunt hoofed animals, such as zebras and wildebeest, so they have developed loose skin on their bellies that protects them from the frantic kicks of their prey.
How do lions feed?
Lions usually eat their prey at the site of the hunt. However, if they kill their prey in the water, they drag it to the ground and then eat it. If they do not finish all the meat at a time, they safeguard it from scavengers and wait for some hours before eating it.
Are Lions loyal to their mate?
Lions live in prides that consist of one primary male lion, several females and one or two lesser males. The primary male mates with his lionesses. Females might also mate with more than one partner. Several females are likely to be in heat at the same time.
Are Lions loyal?
The lion will definitely kill the person who raised him since birth exactly because he is so loyal to him. Lions are nothing but big cats. They behave like one too.

Revised text:

Lions exhibit a variable feeding pattern dictated by prey availability and individual needs. Typically, lions consume between 5 and 7 kilograms (11-15 pounds) of meat every three to four days. However, they can endure periods of up to two weeks without food, subsequently consuming massive meals—up to 50 kilograms (110 pounds), nearly a quarter of their body weight—in a single feeding.

Daily caloric requirements for lions fluctuate considerably. Wild lions generally consume between 4.5 and 11.3 kilograms (10-25 pounds) daily. Captive lions, managed by organizations like the Association of Zoos and Aquariums, require slightly less, with male lions receiving approximately 3.6 kilograms (8 pounds) and females receiving around 2.7 kilograms (6 pounds) per day.

Lions are opportunistic feeders, often consuming their kill at the site of the hunt. When prey is killed in water, lions will transport it to dry land before feeding. To protect remaining meat from scavengers, lions frequently cache their kills and resume eating several hours later.

Beyond sustenance, lions’ physical attributes and social structures contribute to their survival. Their tawny fur provides camouflage in savanna, woodland, and desert habitats. Loose skin on their abdomens protects them from the defensive kicks of prey like zebras and wildebeest, which constitute their primary food source. 

Lions live in prides, typically consisting of a dominant male, several females, and occasionally subordinate males. While the dominant male primarily sires offspring, females may also mate with multiple males, and several females often cycle into heat simultaneously.

How to Remove .Php Extension From A URL In Nginx?
12 minutes read
To remove the .php extension from a URL in Nginx, you can use the following configuration in your Nginx server block:

location / {
 try_files $uri $uri/ $uri.php?$args;
}
location ~ \.php$ {
 include snippets/fastcgi-php.conf;
 fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php7.4-fpm.sock;
 fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name;
 include fastcgi_params;
}
Explanation:
 1. The location / block checks for the existence of requested files or directories. It first tries to serve the requested URL as is ($uri), then it checks if a directory with the same name exists ($uri/), and finally, it appends .php to the URL and passes the arguments ($uri.php?$args) if the previous checks fail.
 2. The location ~ \.php$ block is responsible for handling PHP files. It includes the fastcgi-php.conf configuration snippet to ensure proper PHP-FPM handling. The fastcgi_pass directive specifies the PHP-FPM socket file, which may vary depending on your PHP version/configuration.
 3. The fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name; line sets the path to the PHP script file based on the document root and the requested script name. This ensures that PHP-FPM knows which file to execute.
 4. The include fastcgi_params; line includes additional FastCGI parameters that are typically required by PHP-FPM.
By utilizing this Nginx configuration, the .php extension will be automatically removed from the URLs while allowing the server to correctly handle the PHP files.

How can we redirect URLs without the .php extension to the corresponding URL with the extension?
To redirect URLs without the .php extension to the corresponding URL with the extension, you can use an Apache .htaccess file or server-side scripting.
 1. Using .htaccess file (Apache server): Create or modify the .htaccess file in the root directory of your website if it doesn't already exist. Make sure the mod_rewrite module is enabled on your server. Add the following lines to your .htaccess file: RewriteEngine On RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-d RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME}.php -f RewriteRule ^(.*)$ $1.php [NC,L] Save the .htaccess file and upload it to your server.
 2. Using server-side scripting (e.g., PHP): Add the following code to the top of your PHP file to check if the requested file with .php extension exists, and if not, redirect to the URL with the extension: Save the PHP file and upload it to your server.
Both approaches will redirect requests for URLs without the .php extension to the corresponding URL with the extension, ensuring a seamless user experience while maintaining proper URLs.
What does the .php extension in a URL indicate?
The .php extension in a URL indicates that the webpage is written in PHP (Hypertext Preprocessor) scripting language. PHP is a widely-used server-side scripting language that is primarily used for web development. When a web server receives a request with a .php extension, it recognizes that it needs to process the PHP code before sending the HTML response to the client's browser.
How can we open the nginx configuration file?
To open the nginx configuration file, you can follow these steps:
 1. Open a terminal or command prompt.
 2. Navigate to the directory where the nginx configuration files are located. The default location is usually /etc/nginx/. You may need root/administrator privileges to access this directory.
 3. Use a text editor to open the configuration file. Here are a few common options: Nano: sudo nano nginx.conf Navigate through the file using the arrow keys. Press Ctrl + O to save changes, and Ctrl + X to exit the editor. Vim: sudo vim nginx.conf Press i to enter insert mode, make changes, press Esc to exit insert mode, then type :wq and press Enter to save changes and exit Vim. GNU Emacs: sudo emacs nginx.conf Use the Emacs commands for editing, saving, and exiting the file. Sublime Text: sudo subl nginx.conf If Sublime Text is installed, it will open the file in a new window where you can make changes. Save the file and close the window to exit. VSCode: sudo code nginx.conf If Visual Studio Code is installed, it will open the file in a new window where you can make changes. Save the file and close the window to exit. Note: You can use any text editor you prefer.
Remember to use sudo (Super User) or have appropriate permissions to access and modify the nginx configuration file.
